g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Creating Multiple Threads in Java, a fundamental concept for concurrent programming.
Multithreading enables Java programs to execute multiple tasks concurrently using lightweight threads that share memory space.
g.text: threads run concurrently within a program.

g.data-narration: Multithreading improves program responsiveness, increases throughput by using multiple CPU cores, and enhances user experience.
The Runnable interface defines a single run() method containing the code to be executed by a thread - the preferred approach.
g.text: Defines a single method run() that contains the task code.

g.data-narration: The Thread class wraps a Runnable object and provides methods to control thread execution like start() and join().
g.text: Wraps the Runnable object and provides methods to control execution.
Thread t = new Thread(task); // Creates Thread object, NOT new execution thread

g.data-narration: A thread goes through four stages: Creation, Start, Execution, and Termination. Each stage has specific methods and behaviors.
Thread priorities determine execution order. Higher priority threads get scheduled before lower priority ones, ranging from 1 to 10.
g.text: Determine execution order. Higher priority threads execute first.

g.data-narration: Synchronization coordinates thread execution to prevent data inconsistencies when multiple threads access shared resources.
g.text: Coordinates execution of multiple threads to prevent data inconsistencies.
// only one thread at a time

g.data-narration: Step 1: Define a class that implements the Runnable interface with the run() method containing the task code.
g.text: Define a class that implements Runnable interface:

g.data-narration: Step 2: Create Runnable task objects and pass them to Thread constructors to create multiple thread instances.
g.text: Step 2: Create Runnable and Thread Objects
Create Runnable objects and pass them to Thread objects:

g.data-narration: Step 3: Call start() on each Thread object to create new execution threads. Never call run() directly - it runs in main thread.
g.text: Call start() to create new execution threads (NEVER call run() directly):

g.data-narration: Step 4: Use join() method to block the main thread until all created threads complete their execution.
g.text: Use join() method to wait for all threads to complete:

g.data-narration: Runnable interface is preferred over extending Thread class due to Java's single inheritance limitation and better reusability.
g.text: Runnable Interface vs Extending Thread Class

g.data-narration: This complete example demonstrates creating three concurrent threads that each print messages with a sleep delay.
Remember: Use Runnable interface, always call start() not run(), handle InterruptedException, and use join() for synchronization.
Final exam tips: threads may execute in any order, be prepared for output prediction questions, and understand thread priorities.
g.text: 1. Threads may execute in any order - be prepared for output prediction questions
2. join() is commonly asked in exams for synchronizing thread completion
3. Don't forget to handle InterruptedException when using sleep() or join()
